title: Felting a gradient roving blend
subtitle: Blending two or more roving colours into a seamless fade before felting
excerpt: Blend two contrasting colours of merino roving into a smooth gradient by hand-teasing the fibres at the colour boundary. The blended roving can then be used in any wet-felting or needle-felting project to produce a seamless fade rather than a hard colour seam.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting and roving-preparation practice. Gradient blending by hand-teasing is documented in British felting guild teaching as an intermediate colour-work technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. For gradient blending, two roving colours are teased together at their meeting point to create a transitional zone.
Mixing two or more colours of fibre together so they blend visually rather than sitting as separate blocks of colour.
A smooth transition from one colour to another, achieved by progressively increasing one colour while decreasing the other across the blending zone.

content.text: before felting means the final piece has a fade rather than a hard colour boundary. The technique is the same whether you use it in flat wet-felting, in a 3D form, or in needle felting: the two colours are
by hand-teasing at the boundary zone. The width of the transition is yours to control by the length of the overlapping zone.
Step 1: Pull the two colours to the same weight

Pull each roving colour into a length of roughly equal weight. For a flat panel about 30 cm square you will need about 30 g total. Colour A provides the start colour; colour B provides the end colour; the blending zone will be the middle section.
Step 2: Overlap and tease the boundary

Lay the end of colour A and the beginning of colour B overlapping by about 5 cm. Tease the two overlapping sections together by pulling them apart slightly and letting the fibres intermingle, then pressing them back together. Repeat this pulling-apart and pressing three or four times until the fibres are visibly mixed at the boundary. For a longer, softer fade, use a 10 to 12 cm overlap zone; for a sharper transition, use a 3 cm zone.
Step 3: Layer the blended roving into the project'

Use the blended length as you would any roving in the project. For a flat panel, lay the gradient horizontally across the panel, with colour A at one edge and colour B at the other, with the blended zone in between. For a 3D form, wrap the gradient around the form so the fade runs in the direction you want (e.g. top to bottom on a bowl from white to indigo).

Three-colour gradients work the same way: blend A into B, then B into C. Keep each blending zone the same length for visual consistency. For a circular gradient in a flat panel, lay the colours in concentric rings and blend each ring boundary.

items.problem: The blended zone looks like a sharp stripe rather than a fade
items.cause: The overlapping zone was not teased enough times for the fibres to fully intermingle
items.fix: Pull the boundary section apart and press together five or six times rather than three. The more passes, the softer the fade.

items.problem: The gradient disappeared after felting and the two colours read as separate blocks
items.cause: During the felting process, the blending zone was laid too thin so the individual colour layers showed through as stripes
items.fix: When laying the blended section into a flat panel, use three thin layers through the gradient zone rather than one thick layer, to reinforce the blend.
